# 2. Data

We utilize a county-level unbalanced panel dataset of alfalfa production outcomes (yield, area, and production output), livestock inventory, and weather variables constructed from multiple sources. The county-level alfalfa production outcomes and livestock inventory data are sourced from the USDA National Agricultural Statistics Service (NASS) Quick Stats database [@usda_quickstats]. Weather variables are derived from the Parameter-elevation Regressions on Independent Slopes Model (PRISM) weather dataset, which provides grid-level (2.5 x 2.5 mile) daily weather data processed according to related literature [@schlenker2009; @tack2015]. Degree-day variables (detailed in Section 3) are calculated based on the interpolation procedure [@schlenker2009].

All weather variables were first aggregated to the county level as the weighted mean of all the PRISM grids within each county, using the proportion of each PRISM grid related to alfalfa and pasture cover based on cropland data layers (CDLs) from NASS CropScape (2008-2022) [@cropscape] as the aggregation weights. Subsequently, the daily degree days and precipitation were further aggregated over each year's growing season for each county. The growing seasons are defined by state according to the NASS definition of the marketing year for hay.^[Marketing-year windows: Arizona and California, April 1 to March 31; Kansas, Kentucky, Louisiana, Mississippi, Missouri, Nevada, New Mexico, North Carolina, Oklahoma, Pennsylvania, South Carolina, Tennessee, Texas, Utah, and Virginia, May 1 to April 30; and all other states, June 1 to May 31.] Within each state-defined range, we aggregated data only over the first `r objs$window_selection$chosen_months` months.^[All data and models were processed on Beocat, a high-performance computing cluster at Kansas State University (<https://beocat.ksu.edu/>).]

Table 1 presents summary statistics of the data, which spans `r format(objs$summary$n_counties, big.mark = ",")` counties. On average, counties have `r fmt_num(objs$summary$area_mean, 2)` thousand acres devoted to alfalfa cultivation, yielding a production output of `r fmt_num(objs$summary$prod_mean, 2)` thousand tons. The average yield of alfalfa stands at `r fmt_num(objs$summary$yield_mean, 2)` tons per acre, indicating a productive use of the land under typical conditions. Boxplot of the yield data shown in Figure 1 (panel a) exhibits significant temporal and spatial variation. Weather conditions during the first `r objs$window_selection$chosen_months` months of the alfalfa growing season include an average precipitation of `r fmt_num(objs$summary$ppt_mean, 2)` inches. Temperature accumulation, measured in degree-days, shows varied growing conditions: `r fmt_num(objs$summary$dd1_mean, 2)` degree-days are recorded below `r fmt_degC(objs$knots$lower)`; `r fmt_num(objs$summary$dd2_mean, 2)` degree-days between `r fmt_degC(objs$knots$lower)` and `r fmt_degC(objs$knots$upper)`, an optimal range for alfalfa (as shown in section 3); and only `r fmt_num(objs$summary$dd3_mean, 2)` degree-days above `r fmt_degC(objs$knots$upper)`, indicating exposure to heat that could stress the plants. Boxplots of all the weather variables are shown in Figure 1 panels (b) – (e) and they exhibit significant temporal and spatial variations.

Figure 2, panels (a)-(c), depicts the spatial distribution of county-level alfalfa production metrics and cattle inventory across the US, focusing on the period from 2002 to 2022. To enhance the spatial coverage, we incorporated missing county-level data from the US agricultural censuses conducted in 2002, 2007, 2017, and 2022. Alfalfa production is dominated by Western U.S., where counties report exceptionally high average yield of `r fmt_num(objs$spatial$region$West$yield, 2)` tons per acre on an average of `r fmt_num(objs$spatial$region$West$area, 2)` thousand acres per county. Key contributors exhibiting significant alfalfa (area [1000 acres], production [1000 tons], yield [tons per acre])^[Each of the three figures is an independent county-level average over 2002–2022 (mean area, mean production, and mean yield); average area multiplied by average yield therefore need not equal average production.] include Arizona (`r fmt_num(objs$spatial$state$AZ$area,2)`, `r fmt_num(objs$spatial$state$AZ$production,2)`, `r fmt_num(objs$spatial$state$AZ$yield,2)`), California (`r fmt_num(objs$spatial$state$CA$area,2)`, `r fmt_num(objs$spatial$state$CA$production,2)`, `r fmt_num(objs$spatial$state$CA$yield,2)`), Idaho (`r fmt_num(objs$spatial$state$ID$area,2)`, `r fmt_num(objs$spatial$state$ID$production,2)`, `r fmt_num(objs$spatial$state$ID$yield,2)`), Nevada (`r fmt_num(objs$spatial$state$NV$area,2)`, `r fmt_num(objs$spatial$state$NV$production,2)`, `r fmt_num(objs$spatial$state$NV$yield,2)`), and Utah (`r fmt_num(objs$spatial$state$UT$area,2)`, `r fmt_num(objs$spatial$state$UT$production,2)`, `r fmt_num(objs$spatial$state$UT$yield,2)`). Western counties are followed by those in the Midwest. Leading Midwestern states include South Dakota (`r fmt_num(objs$spatial$state$SD$area,2)`, `r fmt_num(objs$spatial$state$SD$production,2)`, `r fmt_num(objs$spatial$state$SD$yield,2)`), Wisconsin (`r fmt_num(objs$spatial$state$WI$area,2)`, `r fmt_num(objs$spatial$state$WI$production,2)`, `r fmt_num(objs$spatial$state$WI$yield,2)`), North Dakota (`r fmt_num(objs$spatial$state$ND$area,2)`, `r fmt_num(objs$spatial$state$ND$production,2)`, `r fmt_num(objs$spatial$state$ND$yield,2)`), Nebraska (`r fmt_num(objs$spatial$state$NE$area,2)`, `r fmt_num(objs$spatial$state$NE$production,2)`, `r fmt_num(objs$spatial$state$NE$yield,2)`), and Minnesota (`r fmt_num(objs$spatial$state$MN$area,2)`, `r fmt_num(objs$spatial$state$MN$production,2)`, `r fmt_num(objs$spatial$state$MN$yield,2)`). At the low end, Southern counties exhibit much smaller alfalfa areas. The lowest performing Southern counties are found in Mississippi (`r fmt_num(objs$spatial$state$MS$area,2)`, `r fmt_num(objs$spatial$state$MS$production,2)`, `r fmt_num(objs$spatial$state$MS$yield,2)`), South Carolina (`r fmt_num(objs$spatial$state$SC$area,2)`, `r fmt_num(objs$spatial$state$SC$production,2)`, `r fmt_num(objs$spatial$state$SC$yield,2)`), Georgia (`r fmt_num(objs$spatial$state$GA$area,2)`, `r fmt_num(objs$spatial$state$GA$production,2)`, `r fmt_num(objs$spatial$state$GA$yield,2)`), Louisiana (`r fmt_num(objs$spatial$state$LA$area,2)`, `r fmt_num(objs$spatial$state$LA$production,2)`, `r fmt_num(objs$spatial$state$LA$yield,2)`), and North Carolina (`r fmt_num(objs$spatial$state$NC$area,2)`, `r fmt_num(objs$spatial$state$NC$production,2)`, `r fmt_num(objs$spatial$state$NC$yield,2)`).
